title: Corporations & LLCs
subject: Corporations
summary: Corporations and LLCs covers formation, promoters, stock, shareholder voting, derivative suits, veil piercing, fiduciary duties, mergers, dissolution, securities rules, and LLC rules.

sections.title: Big Picture
body: Corporations law is about how a corporation is formed, who controls it, who owns it, who owes duties, and who is liable when things go wrong.
The main bar exam questions usually ask:
Who had authority, who owed a duty, who can sue, and who is personally liable?

sections.title: 1. What Is a Corporation?
body: A corporation is a legal entity that is separate from its owners.
Continue existing even if shareholders change
The corporation is legally separate from the people who own and run it.
A corporation is like a separate legal person wearing a business suit.
The shareholders own the suit, but the corporation itself signs contracts, owns property, and owes debts.

sections.title: 2. Main Players in a Corporation
body: Directors manage the corporation at the big-picture level.
Employees are agents of the corporation and act within their authority.
Shareholders own. Directors manage. Officers operate.

sections.title: 3. Formation of a Corporation
body: To form a corporation, the incorporator must file articles of incorporation with the state.
The articles are also called the:
Name and address of registered agent
Name and address of each incorporator
The corporate name usually must include words like:
The articles may state the corporation’s purpose.
A broad purpose is valid, such as:
A corporation usually has perpetual existence unless the articles say otherwise.
Corporation starts by filing articles with the state.

sections.title: 4. Incorporator
body: An incorporator is the person who signs and files the articles of incorporation.
An incorporator is not automatically liable for promoter contracts just because the incorporator filed the articles.
Incorporator files the papers. Promoter makes the pre-formation deals.

sections.title: 5. Promoters
body: A promoter is a person who acts before the corporation exists to bring the corporation into existence.
Maya wants to start a corporation.
Before the corporation exists, Maya signs a lease for office space “on behalf of Future Tech Inc.”

blocks.text: A promoter is a person who acts before the corporation exists to bring the corporation into existence.

sections.title: 6. Promoter Liability
body: A promoter is personally liable for pre-incorporation contracts if the promoter knowingly acted for a corporation that did not yet exist.
The promoter remains liable even after the corporation is later formed, unless there is a novation.
A novation is a new agreement where the third party agrees to release the promoter and look only to the corporation.
Maya signs a lease before Future Tech Inc. exists.
Maya is still personally liable on the lease unless the landlord agrees to release Maya and accept only Future Tech Inc. as liable.
If the third party knew the corporation did not yet exist and agreed to look only to the future corporation, the promoter may not be personally liable.
Promoter signs before corporation exists = promoter usually personally liable unless novation.

sections.title: 7. Corporation Liability for Pre-Incorporation Contracts
body: A corporation is not automatically liable for contracts entered before it existed.
Because the corporation did not exist yet.
It could not authorize the contract.
A corporation becomes liable if it adopts the contract after incorporation.
The board formally approves the contract.
The corporation accepts the benefits of the contract.
Maya signs a lease before Future Tech Inc. exists.
After incorporation, Future Tech Inc. moves into the office and pays rent.
Future Tech Inc. likely adopted the lease.
Adoption makes the corporation liable, but it does not automatically release the promoter.
The promoter needs a novation to be released.
Corporation liable after adoption. Promoter released only by novation.

sections.title: 8. Promoter Fiduciary Duties
body: A promoter owes fiduciary duties to the future corporation.
The promoter cannot secretly profit from pre-incorporation transactions.
Then Maya sells it to the new corporation for $200,000 without disclosing the profit.
Maya may have breached a fiduciary duty.
Promoter cannot secretly profit from the corporation being born.

sections.title: 9. De Jure Corporation
body: A de jure corporation exists when all statutory incorporation requirements have been satisfied.
Once a de jure corporation exists, the corporation is liable for corporate obligations.
Shareholders, directors, and officers are generally not personally liable merely because of their roles.
De jure corporation = properly formed corporation.

sections.title: 10. Defective Incorporation
body: Sometimes people think they formed a corporation, but something went wrong.
Are the people personally liable, or will the law still treat the business like a corporation?

blocks.text: Sometimes people think they formed a corporation, but something went wrong.

sections.title: 11. No Good-Faith Effort to Incorporate
body: If people conduct business as a corporation without trying to comply with incorporation requirements, they are personally liable for obligations incurred.
Maya calls her business “Maya Corp.” but never files articles and never tries to incorporate.
No filing effort = no corporate shield.

sections.title: 12. De Facto Corporation
body: A de facto corporation is a business treated like a corporation even though incorporation was defective.
Made a good-faith effort to incorporate
Had a valid law under which they could incorporate
The owners may avoid personal liability.
Many RMBCA jurisdictions have abolished this doctrine.
De facto corporation = tried in good faith, acted corporate, but filing failed.

sections.title: 13. Corporation by Estoppel
body: A person who deals with a business as if it were a corporation may be stopped from later denying corporate existence to hold the owner personally liable.
Corporation by estoppel usually applies only in contract cases.
A supplier signs a contract with “Future Tech Inc.” and treats it like a corporation.
Later, the supplier discovers incorporation was defective.
The supplier may be estopped from saying the corporation did not exist.
If you treated it like a corporation, you may be stuck treating it like one.

sections.title: 14. Ultra Vires Acts
body: An ultra vires act is an act outside the corporation’s stated purpose or powers.
Ultra vires acts are usually not void.
They can generally be challenged only by:
A shareholder seeking to enjoin the act
The corporation suing the director, officer, or employee who acted improperly
The state seeking to enjoin the act
A corporation’s articles say it may only operate bookstores.
The corporation starts running a restaurant.
A third party generally cannot avoid liability by saying the corporation acted outside its powers.
Ultra vires is rarely a contract escape card today.

sections.title: 15. Stock: Big Picture
body: Stock represents ownership in the corporation.
A corporation generally must have stock that:
Carries voting rights for corporate governance
Common stock usually carries ordinary ownership and voting rights.
Preferred stock usually has preference over common stock for distributions or liquidation.
Common stock = basic ownership. Preferred stock = special payment priority.

sections.title: 16. Issuance of Stock
body: A corporation may issue stock if the articles authorize it.
Usually, the board of directors must approve the issuance.
A corporation may accept different types of consideration, including:
Once the corporation receives proper consideration, the stock is generally:
This means the shareholder cannot usually be forced to pay more for that stock.
Corporation can issue authorized stock for valid consideration.

sections.title: 17. Par Value and Watered Stock
body: Par-value stock is stock assigned a stated minimum value.
If stock has par value, the corporation must receive at least that value.
Watered stock is stock issued for less than par value.
Under modern statutes, once the board determines consideration is adequate, courts usually do not second-guess the value.
Par value = minimum stated price. Watered stock = sold below par.

sections.title: 18. Stock Subscriptions
body: A stock subscription is a promise to buy stock.
A pre-incorporation stock subscription is usually irrevocable for six months unless:
The subscription agreement says otherwise; or
If a subscriber does not pay, the corporation may:
Sell the stock to someone else after proper demand
Pre-incorporation stock subscription usually locked in for six months.

sections.title: 19. Preemptive Rights
body: Preemptive rights allow existing shareholders to buy new shares before outsiders so they can maintain their ownership percentage.
A and B each own 50% of a corporation.
If A and B have preemptive rights, each can buy 10 shares to keep their 50% ownership.
Under the RMBCA, shareholders do not have preemptive rights unless the articles provide for them.
Preemptive rights usually do not apply to shares:
Issued within six months of incorporation if authorized in the articles
Sold for property instead of money
Preemptive rights protect ownership percentage, but only if the articles give them.

sections.title: 20. Distributions
body: A distribution is a transfer of money or property from the corporation to shareholders.
The most common distribution is a:
The board of directors authorizes distributions.
Shareholders generally cannot force the board to declare dividends.
A court may compel a dividend if:
Directors acted in bad faith by refusing to declare it.
Board decides dividends unless bad faith abuse is shown.

sections.title: 21. Limits on Distributions
body: A corporation may not make a distribution if it is insolvent or if the distribution would make it insolvent.
The corporation must be able to pay its debts as they come due.
The corporation’s assets must exceed its liabilities plus liquidation preferences.
For dividends, solvency is measured when the dividend is declared.
For stock purchases, solvency is measured when the purchase price is paid.
No distributions if the corporation cannot pay debts or balance its books.

For stock purchases, solvency is measured when the purchase price is paid.

sections.title: 22. Director Liability for Unlawful Distributions
body: A director who votes for or assents to an unlawful distribution may be personally liable to the corporation.
A liable director can seek contribution from other liable directors.
A liable director may recover from shareholders who knowingly accepted an unlawful distribution.
Director who approves illegal payout may have to pay it back.

sections.title: 23. Stock Dividends and Stock Splits
body: A stock dividend or stock split gives shareholders more shares instead of cash or property.
Stock dividends usually are not treated as distributions because they do not change corporate assets or liabilities.
A corporation gives each shareholder one extra share for every share owned.
That is a stock dividend or stock split.
Stock dividend changes paper ownership, not corporate assets.

sections.title: 24. Restrictions on Sale of Stock
body: A shareholder is usually free to sell stock.
But restrictions are common in closely held corporations.
A transfer restriction is generally enforceable against a buyer only if the buyer knows of it.
For certificated shares, the restriction should be conspicuously noted on the stock certificate.
Total bans on transfer are more vulnerable to challenge.
Stock is usually transferable, but close corporations often restrict transfers.

sections.title: 25. Federal Securities Registration
body: Public offerings of securities generally must be registered with the SEC.
The corporation must usually provide a prospectus.
If registration rules are violated, a purchaser may sue for:
Compensatory damages for material misstatements or omissions
Public securities offerings usually need SEC registration.

sections.title: 26. Rule 10b-5
body: Rule 10b-5 is the main federal securities fraud rule.
It deals with fraud in connection with buying or selling securities.
A private plaintiff usually must show:
Plaintiff bought or sold a security
Defendant engaged in fraudulent or deceptive conduct
10b-5 = securities fraud in purchase or sale.

sections.title: 27. Buyer or Seller Requirement
body: A private damages plaintiff must have bought or sold a security.
A person who merely decided not to buy or sell usually cannot recover damages under Rule 10b-5.
A forced exchange of shares in a merger may count as a sale.
No buy or sell = usually no private 10b-5 damages claim.

sections.title: 28. Interstate Commerce Requirement
body: The transaction must involve interstate commerce.
This requirement is easy to satisfy.
Phone, mail, email, or exchange usually satisfies interstate commerce.

sections.title: 29. Fraudulent or Deceptive Conduct
body: Rule 10b-5 covers false or misleading statements in connection with securities transactions.
Making an untrue statement of material fact
Omitting a fact needed to make statements already made not misleading
A pure omission is usually not enough unless disclosure is needed to make something already said accurate and not misleading.
An opinion or prediction may be fraudulent if:
It was not made in good faith
Cautionary language can protect forward-looking statements.
10b-5 punishes misleading securities statements, not every silence.

sections.title: 30. Insider Trading
body: A person with material nonpublic information must either:
Merely possessing inside information is not enough.
The person must trade based on it.

blocks.text: A person with material nonpublic information must either:
Merely possessing inside information is not enough.

sections.title: 31. Insiders
body: An insider is a director, officer, or employee who uses nonpublic information for personal gain.
A CFO learns the company will miss earnings and sells stock before the news becomes public.
Insider = corporate person trading on secret corporate information.

blocks.text: An insider is a director, officer, or employee who uses nonpublic information for personal gain.

Insider = corporate person trading on secret corporate information.

sections.title: 32. Constructive Insiders
body: A constructive insider is an outsider who has access to confidential corporate information because of a relationship with the corporation.
A law firm attorney learns confidential merger information and trades on it.
That attorney may be a constructive insider.
Constructive insider = outsider temporarily trusted with inside information.

sections.title: 33. Tippees
body: A tippee receives inside information from an insider or constructive insider.
A tippee may be liable if:
The tipper breached a duty by giving the tip
The tipper received a personal benefit or intended a gift
The tippee knew or should have known of the breach
A CEO tells a friend confidential information so the friend can trade.
The friend may be liable as a tippee.
Tippee liability requires dirty tip plus knowledge.

sections.title: 34. Misappropriators
body: A misappropriator uses confidential information to trade securities in breach of a duty owed to the source of the information.
A consultant learns confidential information from a client and trades in the client’s stock.

blocks.text: A misappropriator uses confidential information to trade securities in breach of a duty owed to the source of the information.

sections.title: 35. Materiality
body: A fact is material if a reasonable investor would consider it important in deciding whether to buy or sell securities.
Material = important to a reasonable investor.

blocks.text: A fact is material if a reasonable investor would consider it important in deciding whether to buy or sell securities.

sections.title: 36. Scienter
body: Negligence is not enough for Rule 10b-5.
The defendant must act intentionally or recklessly.
10b-5 needs intent or recklessness, not mere carelessness.

blocks.text: Negligence is not enough for Rule 10b-5.

sections.title: 37. Reliance and Fraud on the Market
body: 37. Reliance and Fraud on the Market
A private plaintiff must show reliance.
If the fraud affected the market price of publicly traded stock, reliance may be presumed.
A corporation issues a false press release.
An investor buys stock at the inflated market price.
The investor may rely on the fraud-on-the-market theory.
Public market price can substitute for direct reliance.

sections.title: 38. Rule 10b-5 Remedies
body: A plaintiff usually gets out-of-pocket damages.
Difference between the stock’s actual value and the price paid or received.
Rescission may be available if the defendant was the buyer or seller in the transaction.
10b-5 damages usually restore the trading loss, not punish.

sections.title: 39. Section 16(b): Short-Swing Profits
body: Certain insiders must return profits from buying and selling company stock within a six-month period.
Shareholders owning more than 10% of a class of stock
President sells stock for $500 per share.
Within six months, President buys stock for $400 per share.
The $100 per share difference may be recoverable by the corporation.
16(b) = insider buys and sells within six months, profit goes back to corporation.

sections.title: 40. Tender Offers
body: A tender offer is an offer to shareholders of a publicly traded corporation to buy their stock at a fixed price, usually above market price.
Tender offers are often used in hostile takeovers.
A person who acquires more than 5% of a class of stock must file a statement with the SEC disclosing:
Tender offer = public offer to buy shares, often for takeover.

sections.title: 41. Articles of Incorporation vs. Bylaws
body: Articles are filed with the state to create the corporation.
They are the corporation’s highest governing document.
Bylaws govern internal management and corporate procedures.
If bylaws conflict with articles, the articles control.

sections.title: 42. Amending Articles
body: If no stock has been issued:
The board may amend if there is a board
Incorporators may amend if there is no board
Articles of amendment are filed with the state
After stock exists, articles usually need board plus shareholders.

blocks.text: If no stock has been issued:

sections.title: 43. Bylaws
body: Bylaws may contain any lawful rules for corporate management that are consistent with the articles.
Who Can Adopt or Amend Bylaws?
Shareholders may limit the board’s power to amend or repeal certain shareholder-approved bylaws.
Bylaws are the corporation’s internal rulebook.

body: An organizational meeting is held after articles are filed.
At this meeting, the corporation may:
Elect directors if incorporators are holding the meeting
Organizational meeting = corporation gets set up after filing.

sections.title: 46. Annual Shareholder Meeting
body: A corporation must hold an annual shareholder meeting.
The main purpose is usually to elect directors.
Other shareholder business may also be addressed.
Failure to hold the annual meeting does not destroy the corporation or invalidate corporate acts.
A shareholder may seek a court order requiring a meeting.

sections.title: 47. Special Shareholder Meeting
body: A special meeting may be called for a specific purpose.
Usually, it may be called by:
Shareholders owning at least 10% of voting shares
The purpose must be stated in the notice.
Special shareholder meeting = specific purpose must be noticed.

If financial reports must be restated due to misconduct, CEO and CFO may have to forfeit incentive pay and stock-sale profits.
CEO and CFO must stand behind public financial reports.

sections.title: 108. Mergers
body: A merger combines two or more corporations so that only one corporation survives.
A consolidation creates a new corporation from the combining corporations.
Required documents are filed with the state
The surviving corporation receives all assets and liabilities of the merged corporation.
Merger = one corporation survives and takes assets plus liabilities.

The surviving corporation receives all assets and liabilities of the merged corporation.

sections.title: 109. Shareholder Approval for Mergers
body: Shareholder approval usually requires a majority vote at a meeting with quorum.
If a class of stock is affected, that class may need separate approval.
Major merger usually needs board plus shareholder approval.

blocks.text: Shareholder approval usually requires a majority vote at a meeting with quorum.
If a class of stock is affected, that class may need separate approval.
Major merger usually needs board plus shareholder approval.

sections.title: 110. Parent-Subsidiary Short-Form Merger
body: A parent corporation owning at least 90% of a subsidiary can usually merge with the subsidiary without subsidiary shareholder approval.
90% parent can usually short-form merge subsidiary.

blocks.text: A parent corporation owning at least 90% of a subsidiary can usually merge with the subsidiary without subsidiary shareholder approval.
90% parent can usually short-form merge subsidiary.

sections.title: 111. Minnow-Whale Merger
body: A minnow-whale merger is a merger of a small corporation into a large corporation.
The large surviving corporation may not need shareholder approval if:
Voting power does not increase by more than 20%
Tiny minnow into huge whale may not need whale shareholder vote.

sections.title: 112. Asset Acquisition
body: Sale of assets in the ordinary course of business usually does not require special shareholder approval.
But transfer of all or substantially all assets outside the ordinary course is a fundamental corporate change.
The transferor remains liable for its debts unless released.
The transferee usually does not assume liabilities unless it agrees.
Ordinary asset sale is easy; sale of substantially all assets needs approval.

sections.title: 113. Stock Acquisition
body: A corporation may gain control of another corporation by buying its stock.
A statutory share exchange may require all shareholders to participate and may give appraisal rights.
Stock acquisition buys control without merging entities.

blocks.text: A corporation may gain control of another corporation by buying its stock.
A statutory share exchange may require all shareholders to participate and may give appraisal rights.
Stock acquisition buys control without merging entities.

sections.title: 114. Dissenting Shareholder Appraisal Rights
body: A shareholder who objects to certain major corporate changes may force the corporation to buy the shareholder’s stock at fair value.
If shares are traded on a liquid and reliable market, appraisal rights may not be available because the shareholder can sell on the market.
Appraisal = dissenting shareholder cashes out at fair value.

Appraisal = dissenting shareholder cashes out at fair value.

sections.title: 115. Appraisal Procedure
body: To preserve appraisal rights, the shareholder usually must:
Give written notice before the vote
Not vote in favor of the action
Accept corporation’s fair value estimate or litigate value
After approval, the corporation sends appraisal notice and form.
The shareholder must return the form within the required window.
To get appraisal, object early and do not vote yes.

sections.title: 116. Voluntary Dissolution
body: A corporation may dissolve by majority vote of incorporators or initial directors.
A corporation may voluntarily dissolve if:
After stock exists, voluntary dissolution needs board plus shareholders.

blocks.text: A corporation may dissolve by majority vote of incorporators or initial directors.
A corporation may voluntarily dissolve if:
After stock exists, voluntary dissolution needs board plus shareholders.

sections.title: 117. Winding Up After Dissolution
body: A dissolved corporation continues to exist for limited purposes.
Selling property not distributed to shareholders
Dissolution starts ending; winding up cleans up.

blocks.text: A dissolved corporation continues to exist for limited purposes.
Selling property not distributed to shareholders
Dissolution starts ending; winding up cleans up.

sections.title: 118. Distribution of Corporate Assets on Dissolution
body: 118. Distribution of Corporate Assets on Dissolution
Assets are distributed in this order:
If a shareholder is also a creditor, that claim may be subordinated if veil-piercing-type facts exist.
Creditors first, preferred shareholders second, common shareholders last.

sections.title: 119. Involuntary Dissolution
body: A court may dissolve a corporation if statutory grounds are shown.
A shareholder may seek dissolution if:
Corporate assets are misapplied or wasted
Directors or controllers act illegally, oppressively, or fraudulently
Directors are deadlocked and harm is threatened
Shareholders are deadlocked and cannot elect directors
The oppression doctrine protects minority shareholders from improper majority control.
Involuntary dissolution protects against deadlock, waste, fraud, and oppression.

sections.title: 120. Administrative Dissolution
body: The state may dissolve a corporation if it:
Fails to pay fees or taxes
Continuing to operate after forfeiture can create personal liability.
State can dissolve corporation for filing/tax failures or abuse.

blocks.text: The state may dissolve a corporation if it:

sections.title: 121. Closely Held Corporation
body: A closely held corporation has only a few shareholders and is not publicly traded.
Close corporation = small group of owners, often running the business themselves.

blocks.text: A closely held corporation has only a few shareholders and is not publicly traded.
Close corporation = small group of owners, often running the business themselves.

sections.title: 122. Foreign Corporation
body: A foreign corporation is a corporation incorporated in another state or jurisdiction.
To do business in another state, it usually must register and obtain a certificate of authority.
Failure to register may prevent the corporation from suing in that state until registered.
Prevent the corporation from being sued
Prevent the corporation from defending lawsuits
The law of the state of incorporation usually governs internal corporate affairs.
Foreign corporation = incorporated elsewhere, must register to do business here.

sections.title: 123. Professional Corporation
body: A professional corporation provides professional services.
The corporation does not protect a professional from liability for their own malpractice.
But it may protect against vicarious liability for malpractice of other professionals.
Professional corporation shields business liability, not your own malpractice.

sections.title: 124. S Corporation
body: An S corporation is a corporation with pass-through taxation.
Income and losses pass through to shareholders.
Shareholders must generally be U.S. citizens or resident aliens
Shareholders must consent to S corporation election
S corp = corporation law plus partnership-style pass-through tax.

sections.title: 125. Benefit Corporation
body: A benefit corporation is a for-profit corporation with a purpose of creating social or environmental benefit.
Directors may be protected when they make decisions advancing the benefit purpose, even if profit is reduced.
Benefit corporation = profit plus social mission.

blocks.text: A benefit corporation is a for-profit corporation with a purpose of creating social or environmental benefit.

sections.title: 126. LLC: Big Picture
body: A limited liability company, or LLC, is a separate business entity that combines:
Pass-through tax advantages of a partnership
The owners of an LLC are called:
LLC = corporation liability shield + partnership flexibility.

blocks.text: A limited liability company, or LLC, is a separate business entity that combines:
Pass-through tax advantages of a partnership
The owners of an LLC are called:
LLC = corporation liability shield + partnership flexibility.

sections.title: 127. Creating an LLC
body: An LLC is created by filing articles or a certificate of organization with the state.
Statement if there are no members at filing
An LLC usually does not exist until at least one person becomes a member.
LLC starts with articles of organization plus at least one member.

blocks.text: An LLC is created by filing articles or a certificate of organization with the state.
Statement if there are no members at filing
An LLC usually does not exist until at least one person becomes a member.
LLC starts with articles of organization plus at least one member.

sections.title: 128. LLC Operating Agreement
body: An operating agreement governs the LLC’s business and member relationships.
The operating agreement usually controls over default statutory rules.
Unless the agreement says otherwise, all current members usually must agree to modify the operating agreement.

blocks.text: An operating agreement governs the LLC’s business and member relationships.
The operating agreement usually controls over default statutory rules.
Unless the agreement says otherwise, all current members usually must agree to modify the operating agreement.

sections.title: 129. LLC Membership
body: All states allow a single-member LLC.
A person usually cannot become a member of a multi-member LLC without the consent of all members.
New LLC member usually needs everyone’s consent.

blocks.text: All states allow a single-member LLC.
A person usually cannot become a member of a multi-member LLC without the consent of all members.
New LLC member usually needs everyone’s consent.

sections.title: 130. Transfer of LLC Interest
body: A member usually cannot transfer full membership rights without consent.
A transferee usually receives only economic rights, such as:
The transferee does not automatically receive:
LLC interest transfer usually gives money rights, not management rights.

blocks.text: A member usually cannot transfer full membership rights without consent.

sections.title: 131. Charging Order
body: A charging order is a court order allowing a creditor to receive distributions that would otherwise go to the LLC member.
The creditor gets economic distributions but does not become a member.
Many statutes treat a charging order as the only way a creditor can reach a member’s LLC interest.
Charging order lets creditor collect distributions, not run the LLC.

sections.title: 132. LLC Profits and Losses
body: The operating agreement controls profits, losses, and distributions.
If the operating agreement is silent, profits and losses are usually allocated based on contributions.
LLC profits follow operating agreement; if silent, follow contributions.

blocks.text: The operating agreement controls profits, losses, and distributions.

sections.title: 133. LLC Management Types
body: Unless the agreement says otherwise, the default is usually member-managed.

blocks.text: Unless the agreement says otherwise, the default is usually member-managed.

sections.title: 134. Member-Managed LLC
body: In a member-managed LLC, members directly manage the business.
Members have broad authority similar to partners.
Each member can usually bind the LLC for ordinary business matters.
Acts outside the ordinary course usually require consent of all members unless the operating agreement says otherwise.
Member-managed LLC = members run and bind the company.

sections.title: 135. Manager-Managed LLC
body: In a manager-managed LLC, managers run the business.
Managers do not need to be members.
Members usually do not have authority to bind the LLC merely because they are members.
Manager-managed LLC = managers run; members usually do not bind.

blocks.text: In a manager-managed LLC, managers run the business.

sections.title: 136. LLC Liability
body: Members and managers are generally not personally liable for LLC obligations merely because of their status.
A member or manager may be personally liable for:
LLC shield protects status liability, not personal wrongdoing.

blocks.text: Members and managers are generally not personally liable for LLC obligations merely because of their status.

sections.title: 137. Piercing the LLC Veil
body: Most states allow veil piercing for LLCs in limited circumstances.
Failure to follow LLC formalities alone is usually not enough.
LLC veil can be pierced for fraud or unfairness, not just informal management.

blocks.text: Most states allow veil piercing for LLCs in limited circumstances.

sections.title: 138. LLC Fiduciary Duties
body: Members in a member-managed LLC and managers in a manager-managed LLC usually owe fiduciary duties.
The operating agreement may modify duties if the modification is not manifestly unreasonable.
LLC managers or managing members owe care and loyalty.

blocks.text: Members in a member-managed LLC and managers in a manager-managed LLC usually owe fiduciary duties.

LLC managers or managing members owe care and loyalty.

sections.title: 139. LLC Duty of Loyalty
body: The duty of loyalty requires the member or manager to:
Account for benefits from LLC activities or property
Avoid acting for an adverse party
A member uses LLC information to secretly take a deal for themselves.
That may breach the duty of loyalty.
LLC loyalty = no secret benefits, adverse dealing, or competition.

sections.title: 140. LLC Duty of Care
body: The duty of care usually requires avoiding:
Good-faith business decisions may be protected.
LLC duty of care usually punishes serious misconduct, not simple mistakes.

blocks.text: The duty of care usually requires avoiding:

sections.title: 141. LLC Dissociation
body: Dissociation means a member leaves the LLC.
A member may dissociate at any time by communicating intent to withdraw.
Dissociation does not automatically cause dissolution.
A dissociated member usually loses the right to participate in management.
Unlike partnership rules, a dissociated LLC member usually does not automatically get paid for their interest unless the operating agreement provides otherwise.
LLC dissociation = member leaves, but LLC usually continues.

sections.title: 142. LLC Direct Actions
body: A member may bring a direct action to enforce the member’s own rights.
The member must show an injury that is not solely the result of harm to the LLC.
The LLC denies a member inspection rights required by the operating agreement.
That may support a direct action.
LLC direct action = member personally harmed.

LLC direct action = member personally harmed.

sections.title: 143. LLC Derivative Actions
body: A member may bring a derivative action on behalf of the LLC for harm to the LLC.
The member usually must make demand on the managers or members unless demand would be futile.
Efforts made to get the LLC to act; or
Reasons why demand was not made
LLC derivative action = member sues for harm to LLC.

sections.title: 144. LLC Dissolution
body: A member may seek court-ordered dissolution if a controlling member acts oppressively and directly harms the member.
LLC dissolution ends the company; dissociation only removes a member.

sections.title: 145. LLC Winding Up
body: After dissolution, the LLC winds up.
The LLC may give notice to known and unknown claimants.
Claims may be enforced against undistributed assets and sometimes against members who received distributions.
LLC winding up = pay debts, close business, distribute leftovers.

body: Ask whether the issue is about:
Is the shareholder protected by limited liability?
Can the corporate veil be pierced?
Did a director or officer breach a duty?
Is a member or manager personally liable for personal misconduct?
Can the LLC veil be pierced?
Did a member or manager breach fiduciary duties?
